source.title: Sacred Geometry Explained Like Never Before

core_synthesis: The source explicitly supports a Flower, Fruit, Seed, Tree cycle as a symbolic organizing grammar; the Egg of Life is tracked as a research bridge while evidence-boundary checks prevent symbolic fit from becoming claim validation.
boundary: This artifact summarizes or maps source material. It does not validate the source claims.
source_boundary: Findings are timestamped paraphrases from the source transcript and metadata. They do not validate the source claims.

cycle_decision.decision: Keep the Egg of Life visible in research/development variants, but keep the public default focused on the source's flower-fruit-seed-tree cycle until contrasting sources clarify whether egg is a peer phase or an embodiment bridge.
rationale: The source explicitly names the Egg of Life, so omitting it from research would lose signal.
The source also gives a clean four-phase fruit-tree metaphor, which is stronger for the package's public front door.
The lab can preserve both views without upgrading symbolic fit into proof.

taxonomy_primary_cycle.interpretive_note: The source gives more than one ordering. The lab should preserve both: the fruit-tree metaphor starts with flower, while the geometry-development sequence starts with seed.

phase_highlights.name: Seed of Life / Genesis pattern
phase_highlights.summary: The source identifies the Seed of Life with the Genesis pattern and maps successive circles to a creation sequence.
phase_highlights.mapping_role: Generative minimum; first complete viable form.

phase_highlights.name: Tree of Life
phase_highlights.summary: The source says the Seed of Life develops into the Tree of Life, then later devotes several chapters to Kabbalah and Tree paths.
phase_highlights.mapping_role: Traversal, lineage, descent, ascent, and ordered relation.

phase_highlights.name: Flower of Life
phase_highlights.summary: The source presents the Flower of Life as a broad creation pattern, a relational bloom, and a symbolic unity form across traditions.
phase_highlights.mapping_role: Expansion into visible relation and patterned abundance.

phase_highlights.name: Egg of Life
phase_highlights.summary: The source explicitly says the Flower of Life yields a fifth image called the Egg of Life and relates it to physical embodiment and early cell structure.
phase_highlights.mapping_role: Embodied seed; transition from symbolic cycle to biological/body analogy.

phase_highlights.name: Bounded 19-circle Flower of Life
phase_highlights.summary: The source notes the Flower of Life is often depicted with 19 circles and compares the larger boundary circles to an egg-like protective layer.
phase_highlights.mapping_role: Protective boundary; useful for containment and membrane metaphors.

phase_highlights.name: Fruit of Life
phase_highlights.summary: The source presents the Fruit of Life as the result or harvest form from which details of reality are said to be created.
phase_highlights.mapping_role: Distilled complete set; harvest from bloom.

phase_highlights.name: Grid of Life Design / golden triangle
phase_highlights.summary: The later Robert Gilbert material describes a grid practice and a golden triangle as sacred geometry applied to human energy activation.
phase_highlights.mapping_role: Practice-facing geometry; keep outside proof claims.

phase_highlights.name: Point in the center of the circle
phase_highlights.summary: The source treats the center point and surrounding circle as a dense symbolic form for creation, singularity, and manifestation.

phase_highlights.name: Cubicle cross / Cave of Brahma
phase_highlights.summary: The source describes a three-axis cross in the head meeting at the Cave of Brahma and links it to activation of the energy field.

phase_highlights.name: Zero-point centering and radiance
phase_highlights.summary: The source closes with an inward centering and outward radiance pattern, treating sacred geometry as a complete contraction-expansion practice.
phase_highlights.mapping_role: Breath-like movement pattern; useful for dynamic animation and research-loop metaphors.

template_summary.next_action: Build a radial-cycle hybrid map for Experiment 003, then decide whether the public animation should add the Egg of Life as an embodied bridge phase.

audit_summary.next_action: Use the expanded finding set to rerun the template scorecard, then test the hybrid against a contrasting source.

public_next_questions: Can a contrasting source reproduce the same flower-fruit-seed-tree order without importing this source's narrative?
Does the Egg of Life behave like a peer cycle phase, an embodiment bridge, or a derivative boundary image across sources?
Which claims move inward only when independent evidence appears, rather than when symbolic resonance feels strong?
